why did the ancient egyptians call their land kemet, or black land? why did the ancient egyptians call their land kemet, or black land? the ancient egyptians referred to their land as kemet, which translates to "black land," due to the rich, dark soil that surrounded the nile river. this fertile ground, created by the annual flooding of the nile, was capable of supporting a wide variety of crops, including grains, vegetables, and fruits. the black soil was a stark contrast to the beige-colored desert sand that lay beyond the nile's banks, known as deshret or "red land." the distinction between kemet and deshret was not only geographical but also symbolic, as kemet represented the heart of ancient egyptian civilization, where life and fertility thrived.
